content.problemStatement: The Chef's last idea was that some cooks might work better in pairs. But now he thinks that it is better to form one group of Chefs in order to make larger increase of foods quality. All pairs of employees in this group must be compatible. We call such group friendly. Chef provides you the list of compatible pairs of employees. Also for each employee, the Chef has assigned a number estimating how well the overall quality of the food might increase if this employee will be in the group. Strangely enough, the Chef estimates that picking the **i**'th employee in the group will increase the quality of food prepared in his kitchen by exactly **2ci** where ** ci** are nonnegative integer. Also note that Chef has assigned different numbers for his employees. Chef doesn't want to form just one friendly group of employees that make the largest increase of food quality. He wants to have several possibilities of such groups. Namely he wants to know **K** best friendly groups by the value of increase of food quality. Note that Chef considers only non-empty groups. Help him to find these groups.
constraints: All ci are distinct and 0 ≤ ci ≤ 10^9
The graph is simple (no self-loops, no parallel edges)
content.inputFormat: The first line contains an integer T, the number of test cases. Each test case starts with three integers N, M, and K. The next line contains N distinct non-negative integers c0 … cN-1. Then M lines follow, each containing two different integers ui vi (0 ≤ ui, vi < N) describing a compatible pair.
content.outputFormat: For each test case, output K lines. The i-th line must contain the indices of the employees that form the i-th most valuable friendly group (non-empty clique). Indices inside a line have to be printed in increasing order and separated by a single space. After the K lines, output one empty line.

solution.approach: To solve this problem, we can use a brute force approach by generating all possible subsets of the given set of employees and checking if each subset forms a clique. We can use bit manipulation to generate all possible subsets. For each subset, we can check if it forms a clique by verifying if all pairs of employees in the subset are compatible. We can use a priority queue to store the subsets that form a clique and their corresponding values. Finally, we can output the K subsets with the highest values.
solution.timeComplexity: O(2^N * M) per test case, O(T * 2^N * M) total. The time complexity is exponential due to the generation of all possible subsets.
solution.spaceComplexity: O(2^N) — the dominant structure is the priority queue used to store the subsets that form a clique.

code.Python3:
import sys
from queue import PriorityQueue

MAX_N = 10000
MAX_M = 20000
MAX_K = 50

T = int(sys.stdin.readline())
for _ in range(T):
    N, M, K = map(int, sys.stdin.readline().split())
    c = list(map(int, sys.stdin.readline().split()))
    compatible = [[False] * N for _ in range(N)]
    for _ in range(M):
        ui, vi = map(int, sys.stdin.readline().split())
        compatible[ui][vi] = True
        compatible[vi][ui] = True
    # Generate all possible subsets and check if they form a clique
    pq = PriorityQueue()
    for mask in range(1, 1 << N):
        value = 0
        subset = []
        isClique = True
        for i in range(N):
            if (mask & (1 << i)) != 0:
                value += 2 * c[i]
                subset.append(i)
                for j in range(N):
                    if (mask & (1 << j)) != 0 and not compatible[i][j]:
                        isClique = False
                        break
        if isClique:
            pq.put((-value, subset))
    # Output the K subsets with the highest values
    for _ in range(K):
        if pq.empty():
            break
        subset = pq.get()[1]
        print(' '.join(map(str, subset)))
    print()
